target: openclaw/openclaw github_pr #44143: fix: serialize outbound deliveries per channel+recipient
github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 44143 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/44143 - Title: fix: serialize outbound deliveries per channel+recipient - State: OPEN - Author: nicolasgrasset - Labels: channel: mattermost, size: S, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, mantis: telegram-visible-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 message-delivery, merge-risk: 🚨 availability, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Summary When multiple sessions (main + sub-agent) send messages to the same recipient concurrently, replies can arrive out of order because each session's delivery call races independently on the channel's HTTP endpoint. Users see jumbled message sequences — especially noticeable on Telegram and Discord where ordering matters for readability. **Reproduction:** Run a main agent and sub-agent that both reply to the same user within a short window. Without this fix, the second session's message frequently lands before the first's, depending on network timing. **Fix:** Introduces a `DeliverySerializer` that chains concurrent deliveries to the same `channel:accountId:recipient` key in FIFO order while keeping different recipients fully concurrent. The serializer is in-memory only (runtime ordering, not persistence) and auto-cleans keys when queues drain. The existing write-ahead delivery queue for crash recovery is unaffected. A follow-up commit wraps `fn()` in `Promise.resolve().then()` so a synchronous throw is converted to a rejection rather than hanging the result promise forever (addresses review feedback). Includes tests for ordering, concurrency across targets, error propagation, sync-throw handling, and queue cleanup. Rebased to 2026.3.13. ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-12T14:55:52Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR introduces a `DeliverySerializer` — an in-memory per-key async mutex — to guarantee FIFO ordering of outbound messages sent concurrently to the same `channel:accountId:recipient` target, fixing a race condition where sub-agent and main-session deliveries could arrive out of order. - The Promise-chain approach in `DeliverySerializer` is sound: FIFO ordering, error isolation (a failed delivery never blocks subsequent ones), and automatic Map cleanup are all correctly implemented and well-tested. - The integration in `deliver.ts` is minimal and preserves all existing error-handling, partial-failure tracking, and write-ahead queue ACK/NACK logic. - The five-case test suite is deterministic and covers ordering, concurrency, error propagation, cleanup, and stress scenarios. - One latent robustness gap: if `fn()` ever throws synchronously, `resultPromise` will never settle (hanging the caller permanently) and the stored `tail` will produce an unhandled rejection. This cannot happen with the current `async` call-site but is worth guarding against defensively with `Promise.resolve().then(() => fn())`.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- This PR is safe to merge; the mutex logic is correct and the integration change is minimal with no risk to existing error-handling paths. - The Promise-chain mutex pattern is well-understood and correctly implemented. FIFO ordering, error isolation, and cleanup are all verified by tests. The only deduction is for the defensive-programming gap where a synchronously-throwing `fn()` would cause an eternal hang and unhandled rejection — low risk in practice since the call-site is always async, but worth hardening before reuse. - src/infra/outbound/delivery-serializer.ts — review the synchronous-throw robustness concern in the `serialize` method.
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/infra/outbound/delivery-serializer.ts Line: 25-44 Comment: **Synchronous throw from `fn()` causes `resultPromise` to hang forever** If `fn()` throws synchronously (before returning a Promise), the arrow callback inside `prev.then(...)` throws, which causes `tail` to reject. Because neither `resolve` nor `reject` is ever called, `resultPromise` never settles — so `await resultPromise` in this same `serialize` call hangs indefinitely, leaking that Map entry forever. Additionally, the rejecting `tail` stored in the Map has no rejection handler attached, which would produce an unhandled promise rejection warning. In the current call-site `() => deliverOutboundPayloadsCore(wrappedParams)` this can't happen because `deliverOutboundPayloadsCore` is `async`. But a small defensive guard makes the serializer safe for any caller: ```ts // Before storing tail, ensure fn() is always called asynchronously // so a synchronous throw is converted to a rejected Promise and // resolve/reject are always reachable. const runFn = () => Promise.resolve() .then(() => fn()) .then( (v) => { resolve(v); }, (e) => { reject(e); }, ); const tail: Promise = prev.then(runFn, runFn); ``` This ensures `resultPromise` always settles and `tail` always resolves. How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````
Last reviewed commit: bf718f7 - nicolasgrasset at 2026-03-12T15:10:37Z: Closing — need to test locally before submitting. Will reopen after verification. - nicolasgrasset at 2026-03-12T17:05:24Z: Tested in isolated mock environment. E2E results: **With serializer:** 1→2→3→4→5 ✅ (sequential timestamps, ordered delivery) **Without serializer:** 5→4→1→3→2 ❌ (all fire at same timestamp, delivered in random order) Test setup: mock signal-cli HTTP server with random 0-50ms processing delays per request. 5 concurrent sends to same recipient. Serialized sends always arrive in FIFO order; raw concurrent sends arrive scrambled. Unit tests (vitest): 5/5 pass — ordering, concurrency for different targets, error isolation, cleanup, 10-way stress. - nicolasgrasset at 2026-03-12T18:46:19Z: CI failures are all upstream TypeScript errors in `channelContentConfig.ts` and `debug.ts` — unrelated to this PR. The delivery-serializer tests (6/6) pass, and all 220 existing tests pass locally on node + bun. - nicolasgrasset at 2026-03-12T20:40:42Z: Rebased to latest main (2026.3.11). Previous CI failures were upstream TypeScript errors in `channelContentConfig.ts` — unrelated to this PR. All 6 delivery-serializer tests pass locally, plus the full 220-test suite. - nicolasgrasset at 2026-03-12T20:40:55Z: Rebased this branch onto the latest main (2026.3.11) and force-pushed the updated branch. The recent CI failures appear to be upstream TypeScript errors unrelated to this PR. - nicolasgrasset at 2026-03-16T05:02:13Z: The synchronous-throw issue flagged by Greptile was already addressed in ac900bc5e — `fn()` is wrapped in `Promise.resolve().then(() => fn())` so synchronous throws are converted to rejected promises. No additional changes needed. - openclaw-barnacle at 2026-04-28T04:40:06Z: This pull request has been automatically marked as stale due to inactivity. Please add updates or it will be closed. [comments truncated: 2 more] ``` Changed files (5): ```text extensions/mattermost/package.json pnpm-lock.yaml src/infra/outbound/deliver.ts src/infra/outbound/delivery-serializer.test.ts src/infra/outbound/delivery-serializer.ts ```

github_context:

- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/84443
- Title: [Feature]: Add ACP support for Antigravity CLI (agy)
- State: CLOSED
- Author: Openclaw-Metis
- Labels: P3

Body:
```markdown
## Feature request

Please consider adding first-class ACP support for the Antigravity CLI (`agy`) in OpenClaw.

## Why this would be useful

Antigravity CLI is already useful as an interactive coding and generation agent. In local testing, `agy` can:

- Run non-interactively with `agy -p`
- Resume conversations with `agy --conversation=<id>` or `agy -c`
- Switch models through `/model`
- Use its own tools, including image generation
- Access models such as Gemini 3.5 Flash, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Claude Sonnet/Opus 4.6 Thinking, and GPT-OSS 120B

However, OpenClaw currently cannot treat `agy` as a proper ACP-capable agent in the same way it can work with other CLI agents. This makes it harder to use Antigravity as a durable OpenClaw backend for session orchestration, subagent work, inspection, and media/tool workflows.

## Desired behavior

Add an Antigravity CLI ACP integration or bridge so OpenClaw can:

- Start an `agy` session through the normal OpenClaw session/subagent system
- Send prompts and receive structured responses over ACP
- Preserve and resume `agy` conversation IDs
- Expose session status/history in OpenClaw tools
- Support model selection or model override where possible
- Forward attachments/reference images where possible
- Surface generated artifacts, especially image outputs, back to OpenClaw
- Handle long-running `agy` tasks without blocking the main OpenClaw session
- Normalize errors such as auth failure, model unavailability, and tool permission prompts

## Example use cases

- Use `agy` as a selectable subagent runtime from OpenClaw
- Run Antigravity CLI tasks from Discord/Telegram/WhatsApp through OpenClaw
- Generate images through `agy`, then automatically return the output attachment to the chat
- Compare Antigravity model behavior against Codex/Claude agents from the same OpenClaw session
- Let OpenClaw inspect, resume, or terminate stuck `agy` tasks cleanly

## Current workaround

The current workaround is to call `agy -p` or run interactive `agy` manually from a shell, then parse terminal output and locate generated files. This works for experiments, but it is brittle:

- Output paths and artifacts need manual parsing
- Interactive flows can hang
- Session state is not visible to OpenClaw
- No standard ACP-style status/history/tool event stream is available
- Model switching is manual

## Suggested implementation direction

An ACP bridge around `agy` could wrap:

- `agy -p <prompt>` for one-shot execution
- `agy --conversation=<id>` / `agy -c` for resumable sessions
- `agy -i` for interactive mode if needed
- Conversation metadata from `~/.gemini/antigravity-cli/`
- Generated artifacts under `~/.gemini/antigravity-cli/scratch/`

Even a minimal first version that supports one-shot execution, resumable conversation IDs, status reporting, and artifact discovery would make Antigravity much more useful inside OpenClaw.

Thanks for considering this.

```

Body:
```markdown
## Summary
When multiple agent stores exist (e.g. \^Ggents/main\ and \^Ggents/coder\), \openclaw doctor\ reports auth status without identifying which agent's store the result comes from. This makes it impossible to diagnose per-agent auth drift without inspecting files manually.

## Version
OpenClaw 2026.4.23 (a979721)

## Context
OpenClaw stores per-agent auth tokens in separate files:
- \^Ggents/<id>/agent/auth-profiles.json\

When agent stores diverge (e.g. \^Ggents/coder\ has a valid token, \^Ggents/main\ has an expired one), doctor reports the stale state without identifying which agent is the source. This sent debugging in the wrong direction for an extended time.

## Expected Behavior
Doctor output should include the agent id being checked, e.g.:
\\\
Model auth (agent: main)
  - openai-codex:balric@gmail.com: expired
Model auth (agent: coder)
  - openai-codex:balric@gmail.com: ok (expires in 10 days)
\\\

Or at minimum, call out when auth state differs between agent stores.

## Actual Behavior
Doctor reports a single generic auth status line with no indication of which agent's store was checked.

## Impact
Medium — makes debugging per-agent auth drift significantly harder, especially for users with multiple agents (main, coder, operator, etc.)
```

github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 39248
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/39248
- Title: Bug: sandbox.mode: "non-main" silently breaks sessions_spawn subagent initialization
- State: OPEN
- Author: alexputici
- Labels: P1, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-live-repro, impact:session-state, impact:crash-loop, issue-rating: 🐚 platinum hermit

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

When `agents.defaults.sandbox.mode` is set to `"non-main"`, sub-agents spawned via `sessions_spawn` fail to initialize. Sessions are accepted (return valid `childSessionKey` and `runId`) but never start executing — zero tokens, zero messages, no transcript files created. **No errors appear in gateway logs.**

## Environment

- OpenClaw Version: 2026.3.2 (85377a2)
- OS: macOS 15.3.0 (Apple Silicon, arm64)
- Node: v25.8.0
- Docker Desktop: running (coding agent sandbox works fine)

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Set `agents.defaults.sandbox.mode: "non-main"` in config
2. Restart gateway
3. From main agent, spawn a sub-agent:
   ```
   sessions_spawn(task="Reply with: test", model="sonnet")
   ```
4. Check `sessions_history` or `subagents list` after 30+ seconds

## Expected Behavior

Sub-agent starts within seconds, `systemSent: true`, tokens accumulate, task completes.

## Actual Behavior

- `sessions_spawn` returns `status: "accepted"` with valid session key
- Sub-agent appears in `subagents list` as `running`
- `sessions_history` returns `messages: []` indefinitely
- No transcript file created on disk
- `systemSent` never set to `true`
- `totalTokens` never set
- **Zero error output in gateway logs**
- Eventually times out via `runTimeoutSeconds`

## Bisect Results

Systematically tested with a simple one-line task (`Reply with exactly: ...`):

| Test | sandbox.mode | Docker settings | Result |
|------|-------------|----------------|--------|
| 1 | non-main | network: bridge, readOnlyRoot: true | stall |
| 2 | non-main | network: bridge, readOnlyRoot: false | stall |
| 3 | **off** | (same as test 2) | **works (4s)** |
| 4 | non-main | minimal (image + workdir only) | stall |

The issue is **not** caused by specific Docker settings (network, readOnlyRoot, tmpfs). Any `sandbox.mode` that enables Docker for subagents causes silent failure.

## Notes

- **Cron isolated sessions work fine** with `sandbox.mode: "non-main"` on the same config — they appear to use a different code path that bypasses container initialization.
- **Per-agent sandbox overrides are unaffected**: a coding agent with `sandbox.mode: "all"` and its own Docker settings works correctly.
- Possibly related to #5813 (similar symptoms, different attributed cause).
- The silent failure with zero log output makes this very difficult to diagnose.

## Workaround

Set `agents.defaults.sandbox.mode: "off"` and use per-agent `sandbox.mode: "all"` overrides for agents that specifically need sandboxing (e.g., coding agents with `tools.exec.host: "sandbox"`).
```

github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 66252
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/66252
- Title: [Feature]: Per-Agent TTS/STT Configuration Overrides for Multi-Language Support
- State: OPEN
- Author: acwilan
- Labels: enhancement, P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:auth-provider, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
### Summary

Add support for agent-specific text-to-speech (TTS) and speech-to-text (STT) configuration overrides, enabling multiple agents in a single OpenClaw instance to use different voices, languages, or providers.

### Problem to solve

Currently, TTS/STT configuration is global under messages.tts and tools.media.audio. All agents share the same voice, language, and provider settings. This prevents:

• Multi-language agents: Cannot have one agent speak English and another Spanish
• Voice differentiation: Cannot give different agents distinct voices/personalities
• Provider optimization: Cannot use different TTS providers for different use cases (e.g., fast vs. quality)

### Proposed solution

Extend the agent configuration schema to support TTS/STT overrides:

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "list": {
      "spanish-assistant": {
        "systemPrompt": "Eres un asistente útil...",
        "tts": {
          "provider": "macvoice",
          "voice": "Monica",  // Spanish voice
          "rate": 0.5
        },
        "stt": {
          "provider": "deepgram",
          "language": "es"
        }
      },
      "english-assistant": {
        "systemPrompt": "You are a helpful assistant...",
        "tts": {
          "provider": "elevenlabs",
          "voice": "Adam"
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

Implementation notes:

• Overrides merge with global messages.tts config (agent-specific wins)
• Passed to speech providers via SpeechSynthesisRequest.providerOverrides or new agentConfig field
• Optional: Support `` directive tags as runtime override

### Alternatives considered

1. Separate OpenClaw instances: High resource overhead, complex management
2. Model-driven language detection: Unreliable, adds latency, doesn't solve voice differentiation
3. Global config switching: Requires restart, not dynamic per conversation

### Impact

• Users: Enable multi-language households, personalized agents, voice branding
• Plugins: Providers receive richer context; no breaking changes
• Ecosystem: Differentiation feature for OpenClaw vs. competitors

### Evidence/examples

• Discord communities: Spanish + English channels need different agent personalities
• Customer service: Escalation bot (professional voice) vs. casual helper (friendly voice)
• Accessibility: Different voices help users distinguish agent roles

### Additional information

• Related: macvoice plugin supports voice selection; lacks agent context
• Similar features: Agent-specific model already exists—pattern exists
• Priority: Medium—workaround exists (model directives) but is clunky
```

GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 71863 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/71863 - Title: fix(signal): await daemon shutdown on restart - State: OPEN - Author: ZHOUKAILIAN - Labels: channel: signal, size: M, proof: supplied, proof: sufficient, P1, rating: 🐚 platinum hermit, merge-risk: 🚨 availability, status: 👀 ready for maintainer look Body: ```markdown ## Summary - make Signal daemon shutdown asynchronous and await process exit - fall back to SIGKILL if signal-cli does not exit after SIGTERM - add regression coverage for daemon stop semantics and aborted monitor shutdown ## Testing - corepack pnpm exec vitest run extensions/signal/src/daemon.test.ts extensions/signal/src/monitor.tool-result.autostart.test.ts - corepack pnpm exec vitest run extensions/signal/src ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: Signal auto-start monitor shutdown/restart must wait for the spawned daemon process to exit instead of returning while the daemon is still alive, preventing orphaned `signal-cli daemon` processes across abort/restart paths. - Real environment tested: macOS 14.8.5 arm64 on a local OpenClaw checkout, Node v22.22.2, PR head `3fa74e98e72de378366b4dc5d48cae5f6dd21249`. The machine does not have a linked Signal account or `signal-cli` installed, so this proof used the real OpenClaw Signal monitor/daemon lifecycle code with a local signal-cli-compatible child process that exposes `/api/v1/check` and `/api/v1/events`; no external Signal messages were sent. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: ```bash cd /Users/zhoukailian/Desktop/mySelf/openclaw/.worktrees/fix-signal-daemon-stop-race node --import tsx /tmp/openclaw-signal-lifecycle-proof.mjs | tee /tmp/openclaw-signal-lifecycle-proof.out ``` - Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): ```json { "repoHead": "3fa74e98e72de378366b4dc5d48cae5f6dd21249", "baseMain": "e0225380a7454a85db1038e495d18a28b5d918ed", "port": 20031, "pid": 34488, "aliveBeforeAbort": true, "monitorResolved": true, "resolveWaitMs": 659, "aliveAfterMonitorResolved": false, "elapsedMs": 867, "events": "START pid=34488 args=[\"daemon\",\"--http\",\"127.0.0.1:20031\",\"--no-receive-stdout\",\"--receive-mode\",\"manual\"]\nSIGTERM pid=34488 at=1778304230573\nEXIT_AFTER_SIGTERM pid=34488 at=1778304231225", "runtimeLogs": [ { "level": "log", "t": 200, "msg": "signal-cli: signal-cli shim READY pid=34488 http=127.0.0.1:20031" }, { "level": "error", "t": 867, "msg": "signal daemon exited (source=process code=0 signal=null)" } ] } ``` - Observed result after fix: The Signal monitor started the daemon child process, verified it was alive before abort, sent SIGTERM on abort, waited about 659ms for the child to finish its shutdown, then resolved only after the process exited. `aliveAfterMonitorResolved: false` confirms there was no leftover daemon process after `monitorSignalProvider` returned. - What was not tested: A live linked Signal account, real `signal-cli` network traffic, actual inbound/outbound Signal message delivery, or the macOS LaunchAgent restart path. This proof is scoped to the changed OpenClaw daemon lifecycle behavior and avoids sending external Signal messages from the test machine. - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): The regression tests added in this PR demonstrate the previous behavior: on unpatched main, `stop()` returned `undefined` instead of a promise and there was no SIGKILL escalation. The live proof above verifies the patched after-fix runtime behavior on this machine. ## Root Cause - Root cause: Signal daemon shutdown was fire-and-forget. `stop()` only sent SIGTERM and returned immediately, so monitor abort/restart could continue while the old daemon process was still shutting down. - Missing detection / guardrail: There was no regression coverage asserting that daemon stop returns a promise, waits for process exit, is idempotent across overlapping callers, and escalates to SIGKILL when SIGTERM does not finish. - Contributing context (if known): `signal-cli` daemon shutdown can be slow enough that immediate restart paths race with the previous process still owning resources. ## Regression Test Plan - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - `extensions/signal/src/daemon.test.ts` - `extensions/signal/src/monitor.tool-result.autostart.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `stop()` waits for daemon exit after SIGTERM, escalates to SIGKILL after the timeout, reuses an in-flight stop promise, and `monitorSignalProvider` awaits daemon stop before resolving aborted shutdown. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: It exercises the Signal daemon lifecycle seam directly without requiring a live Signal account or sending external messages. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None before this PR. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Signal auto-start restart/shutdown should leave fewer orphaned daemon processes. Shutdown may now wait briefly for `signal-cli daemon` to exit; if it does not exit after SIGTERM, OpenClaw escalates to SIGKILL after the configured timeout. Closes #22676 ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-04-26T01:14:42Z:

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #84707: deleteAfterRun: true does not clean up isolated cron session files
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 84707
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/84707
- Title: deleteAfterRun: true does not clean up isolated cron session files
- State: OPEN
- Author: bottenbenny
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:session-state, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Bug Description

Setting `deleteAfterRun: true` on an isolated cron job does not delete the session `.jsonl` files after the run completes. Session files persist on disk indefinitely, causing disk bloat.

## Environment

- OpenClaw version: 2026.5.12
- Node.js: 22.22.2
- OS: Linux (Ubuntu)

## Configuration

Cron job configured with:
- `sessionTarget: isolated`
- `deleteAfterRun: true`
- `payload.kind: agentTurn`

## Expected Behavior

When `deleteAfterRun: true` is set, the session file (`agents/<agent>/sessions/<run-id>.jsonl`) should be deleted after the cron run completes, regardless of success or failure.

## Actual Behavior

Session files persist on disk. Example:
- File: `agents/main/sessions/8e53f40e-6eda-43b0-9f8d-e1e4e91b7029.jsonl`
- Size: 865K (230 messages)
- Created: 2026-05-20 01:20 (cron run start)
- Still exists: 2026-05-20 23:35 (22+ hours later)

The job had `deleteAfterRun: true` set, but the file was not cleaned up.

## Additional Context

This affects isolated cron jobs that accumulate large contexts (e.g., daily reflection jobs with many tool calls). Without cleanup, sessions grow to 200+ messages, hit context overflow errors, and retry loops burn tokens.

A manual cleanup of `agents/*/sessions/*.jsonl` files older than 24 hours removed 765 files, indicating this is a systemic issue.

## Related

- The docs state: "Isolated jobs run a dedicated agent turn with a fresh session."
- The `deleteAfterRun` flag appears in the cron job schema but may not be wired to session cleanup for isolated runs.
```

- **Cron session entry is persisted before and after execution:** The run persists the cron session entry before execution and again after adopting agent run metadata, so a transcript-backed session can remain if cleanup is not called. - **Existing deleteAfterRun cleanup is delivery-local:** `cleanupDirectCronSessionIfNeeded` calls `sessions.delete` with `deleteTranscript: true`, but it lives inside `dispatchCronDelivery` and is gated by delivery handling. ([`src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-dispatch.ts:749`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/ec8e7003a66f/src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-dispatch.ts#L749), [ec8e7003a66f](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/ec8e7003a66f))

This PR bundles two bug fixes: (1) it detects `tool_use`/`tool_result` history-corruption errors from the Anthropic API and surfaces a friendly recovery message directing users to `/new`, and (2) it ensures the i18n locale is fully loaded before the first render of the Lit application component. **Key changes:** - `errors.ts`: Adds `isToolUseResultMismatchError` / `TOOL_USE_RESULT_MISMATCH_RE` and wires it into both `formatAssistantErrorText` and `sanitizeUserFacingText` (behind the existing `errorContext` guard). - `translate.ts`: Makes `loadLocale` `async` and exposes `i18n.ready: Promise` so callers can await initial locale resolution. - `app.ts`: Introduces a `localeReady` state flag; the component renders `nothing` until both `i18n.ready` and any explicit `setLocale` call from app settings have settled, preventing a flash of untranslated UI. - Tests are added or updated for all three areas. **Issues found:** - The first alternative of `TOOL_USE_RESULT_MISMATCH_RE` is just `tool_use_id`, which matches any string containing that substring. Unrelated validation errors (e.g. `"Invalid format for tool_use_id"`, `"tool_use_id is required"`) will incorrectly display `"Conversation history corruption detected"` — misleading advice that may prompt users to discard their session unnecessarily. - In `app.ts`, `i18n.ready` (reads from `localStorage`/`navigator`) and `settingsLocale` (reads from app settings) are run concurrently via `Promise.all`. If the two locale sources disagree, the non-deterministic resolution order of `setLocale` means the final rendered locale could come from either source.

Confidence Score: 3/5

- Mostly safe to merge; two non-critical issues should be addressed before landing. - The i18n fix is clean and the test improvement is strictly better. The tool-mismatch fix solves a real user-facing problem. However, the overly broad `tool_use_id` regex alternative can cause unrelated validation errors to display an incorrect "history corruption" message, and the concurrent `Promise.all` locale-loading pattern introduces a low-probability but silent race condition when `localStorage` and app settings disagree on the active locale. - `src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers/errors.ts` (regex breadth) and `ui/src/ui/app.ts` (concurrent locale loading).
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers/errors.ts Line: 838-839 Comment: **Overly broad `tool_use_id` alternative in regex** The first branch of the regex — `tool_use_id` — matches any string that merely contains the substring `"tool_use_id"`, with no further constraints. This means error messages completely unrelated to a history-corruption mismatch (e.g. `"Invalid format for tool_use_id"`, `"tool_use_id is required"`, or `"Unsupported tool_use_id type"`) will all return `"Conversation history corruption detected. Please use /new to start a fresh session."`, which is incorrect advice and will confuse users. The other two alternatives (`tool_result.*corresponding.*tool_use` and `unexpected.*tool.*block`) are adequately specific. The first alternative should be tightened to require additional context, for example: ```suggestion const TOOL_USE_RESULT_MISMATCH_RE = /tool_result.*tool_use_id.*not found|tool_result.*corresponding.*tool_use|unexpected.*tool.*block/i; ``` This matches the actual Anthropic API error strings exercised by the tests (`"tool_result block with tool_use_id … not found in the immediately preceding assistant message"`) while avoiding false positives on generic validation errors that happen to reference the field name. How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. --- This is a comment left during a code review. Path: ui/src/ui/app.ts Line: 119-133 Comment: **Potential race condition between `i18n.ready` and `settingsLocale`** Both `i18n.ready` (which loads the locale persisted in `localStorage` / derived from `navigator.language`) and `settingsLocale` (which loads the locale from app `settings`) are kicked off concurrently via `Promise.all`. Because `setLocale` is not re-entrant, whichever resolves *last* wins and sets `this.locale`. If the two sources disagree — e.g. `localStorage` says `"zh-CN"` and `settings.locale` says `"fr"` — the final rendered locale will be whichever translation finishes loading later, which is non-deterministic. App settings should be the authoritative source, so `settingsLocale` ought to be applied *after* `i18n.ready` resolves to avoid being overwritten: ``` i18n.ready .then(() => isSupportedLocale(this.settings.locale) ? i18n.setLocale(this.settings.locale) : Promise.resolve()) .then(() => { this.localeReady = true; }, () => { this.localeReady = true; }); ``` This is a low-probability edge case (requires the two stores to diverge), but it would be an invisible, intermittent bug. How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````

github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_issue - Number: 60381 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/60381 - Title: browser tool: add force parameter for click and expose evaluate action - State: OPEN - Author: Gui-Ni - Labels: P2, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:queueable-fix, clawsweeper:source-repro, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster Body: ```markdown # Browser Tool Enhancement Proposal ## Problem Statement OpenClaw's `browser` tool, built on Playwright, frequently fails to interact with modern frontend frameworks (React/Vue/Svelte) due to strict hit-test and visibility checks during physical click simulation. ### Typical Failure Pattern ``` Error: Element is intercepted - A transparent overlay div is covering the button - The button uses `pointer-events: none` on a parent element - The element has CSS layout that offsets it visually but not in DOM coordinates - Animations/transitions haven't completed before the click is issued ``` This happens because `page.click()` simulates a real physical pointer interaction with full hit-testing, while modern frontend frameworks often build complex DOM structures that look interactive but fail Playwright's strict accessibility/visibility checks. --- ## Proposed Solutions ### Appendix: `javascript:` Goto Hack — Blocked An alternative escape hatch was discovered: using Playwright's `page.goto()` with a `javascript:` URL protocol to inject JS: ```json { "action": "goto", "url": "javascript:document.querySelector('[data-ref=\"e12\"]').click(); void(0);" } ``` **Finding**: ClawX Gateway explicitly rejects this with: ``` GatewayClientRequestError: Navigation blocked: unsupported protocol "javascript:" ``` The Gateway has already anticipated and blocked this vector. This confirms that a proper `evaluate` action must be implemented at the routing level, not workarounded through navigation. --- ## Proposed Solutions ### Solution A: Add `force` Parameter to `click` Action (Low Cost) Playwright natively supports `page.click(selector, { force: true })`, which skips all actionability checks (visibility, stability, animatable, receiving pointer events, etc.). **Implementation**: ```typescript // In browser tool's act handler case "click": const force = params.force === true; await page.click(selector, { force }); break; ``` **When to use**: When the element exists in the DOM but is visually "covered" by transparent layers, or when `pointer-events: none` parent elements block clicks. --- ### Solution B: Add `evaluate` Action (Escape Hatch) Expose Playwright's `page.evaluate()` directly to allow arbitrary JavaScript execution in the page context. **Implementation**: ```typescript case "evaluate": // script: string — JavaScript code to execute in browser context const result = await page.evaluate(params.script); return { success: true, result }; ``` **Capabilities unlocked**: - Direct DOM manipulation to trigger listeners bypassed by physical clicks - Read `window.localStorage` / `sessionStorage` - Extract obfuscated or framework-generated data - Directly call framework methods (e.g., React's `ref.current.click()`) - Bypass CORS restrictions in certain contexts **Example usage**: ```json { "action": "evaluate", "script": "document.querySelector('[data-testid=submit]').click()" } ``` --- ## API Changes ### Extended `act` Action Parameters ```typescript // click subtype { action: "click", ref: "e12", force?: boolean // NEW: skip hit-test and visibility checks } // NEW — evaluate subtype { action: "evaluate", script: string // JavaScript to execute in page context } ``` --- ## Behavior Recommendation | Scenario | Recommended Action | |----------|-------------------| | Simple visible button, no overlays | Default `click` | | Button covered by transparent div | `click` with `force: true` | | Framework-bound event (React `onClick`, Vue `@click`) | `evaluate` with `.click()` | | Need to read `localStorage` / app state | `evaluate` with return statement | | Complex multi-step trigger | `evaluate` with arbitrary JS | --- ## Implementation Pitfalls (暗坑) ### Pitfall 1: `evaluate` Return Value Serialization Playwright's `page.evaluate()` must serialize the return value back to Node.js/Python. **DOM elements cannot be serialized** — attempting to return a raw DOM node will throw or silently return `{}`. | ❌ Wrong | ✅ Correct | |----------|-----------| | `return document.querySelector('.btn')` | `return document.querySelector('.btn').innerText` | | `return window.reactRoot` | `return window.reactRoot.state` | | `return element.getAttribute('href')` | `return element.getAttribute('href')` | **Tool description must include this warning explicitly:** > `The return value of the script must be a primitive type or a JSON-serializable object. Do not return DOM elements or non-serializable objects.` **Implementation**: Add a serialization safety check in the evaluate handler: ```typescript case "evaluate": try { const result = await page.evaluate(params.script); // Verify result is serializable JSON.stringify(result); // will throw if not serializable return { success: true, result }; } catch (e) { return { success: false, error: e.message }; } ``` --- ### Pitfall 2: Model Laziness — `evaluate` Overuse High-intelligence models (GPT-4 class) tend to discover that `evaluate` is a powerful escape hatch and may start using it for **all** interactions instead of attempting normal `click` operations first. This degrades the fidelity of testing (synthetic events behave differently from real user interactions in edge cases). **Consequence**: Interactions that would work fine with `click` might silently fail in production because the model never actually tested the real user flow. **Mitigation — mandatory rule in system prompt:** ``` # 🛡️ Browser Interaction Fallback Rules If your `click` action fails due to "Element is intercepted", "pointer-events: none", or transparent overlays, DO NOT repeat the same click. Immediately adopt one of: 1. **Click the Parent/Child:** Target a structurally adjacent element. The inner ``, ``, or outer wrapper `
` of a button is often not intercepted. 2. **Keyboard Navigation:** If the element is blocked but focusable, click a nearby "safe" area, use `press` action to hit `Tab` until target is focused, then send `Enter` or `Space`. 3. **Scroll to Center:** Intercepted by sticky headers/footers? Attempt to scroll the element to the center of the viewport before clicking. 4. **force: true** (only after the above fail): Use `force: true` as a last-resort retry before falling back to `evaluate`. ``` **The Behavior Recommendation table should be embedded as a hard rule, not a suggestion.** --- ## Risk Assessment ### Security Considerations - `evaluate` allows arbitrary JS execution in the browser context — this is a powerful escape hatch that should be documented as a privileged operation - Suggest marking `evaluate` as a "advanced" or "fallback" action in tool descriptions - The browser profile is already isolated (`browser/openclaw/user-data`), so no cross-session contamination - Serialization validation prevents accidental data leakage through unserializable objects ### Backward Compatibility - Both changes are additive — no existing behavior is modified - `force` defaults to `false` (current behavior unchanged) - `evaluate` is purely new — no conflict with existing actions --- ## Implementation Checklist - [ ] Add `force?: boolean` parameter to click handler - [ ] Add new `evaluate` action case in act handler - [ ] Update tool description/schema to reflect new parameters - [ ] Add documentation in TOOLS.md - [ ] Add test cases for both features --- ## Priority Suggestion **Solution A (`force` parameter)** is trivial to implement and covers the majority of "Element is intercepted" failures. Recommend implementing first. **Solution B (`evaluate` action)** should be added alongside as a proper "escape hatch" for the remaining cases that `force` doesn't solve, and to unlock broader capabilities. ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - clawsweeper at 2026-04-28T17:34:05Z: Codex review: keeping this open for maintainer follow-up; there is still a little grit to resolve. **Workflow note:** Future ClawSweeper reviews update this same comment in place.

**Summary** Keep open: current main already has the requested gated `evaluate` action, but the `click` contract still has no opt-in `force` field or forwarding path. The FSB comments are useful prior art for making forced clicks explicit and auditable rather than a default interaction mode. github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 70002 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/70002 - Title: ci: skip docs sync & translate-trigger workflows in forks - State: OPEN - Author: xudaiyanzi - Labels: size: XS, triage: risky-infra, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, proof: sufficient, P2, rating: 🐚 platinum hermit, merge-risk: 🚨 automation, status: 👀 ready for maintainer look, proof: 📸 screenshot Body: ```markdown ## Summary - **Problem:** Two upstream-only workflows (`docs-sync-publish.yml`, `docs-translate-trigger-release.yml`) fail with `Authentication failed for 'https://github.com/openclaw/docs.git/'` on every push to `main` in any fork, because they rely on the `OPENCLAW_DOCS_SYNC_TOKEN` secret that only exists in `openclaw/openclaw`. - **Why it matters:** Creates a red ✗ on every normal fork-sync (`git fetch upstream main && git push origin main`), confuses new contributors, and spams fork Actions dashboards with irrecoverable failures. - **What changed:** Added `if: github.repository == 'openclaw/openclaw'` to the single job in each of the two workflows — the same guard pattern already used ~30 times across the repo (`ci.yml`, `codeql.yml`, `control-ui-locale-refresh.yml`). - **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No changes to workflow logic, secrets, the publish repo, the sync script (`scripts/docs-sync-publish.mjs`), documentation, or any other workflow. Behavior in `openclaw/openclaw` is byte-for-byte identical. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [x] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [x] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause (if applicable) - **Root cause:** The two workflows were added without the fork-guard expression that the rest of the CI already uses. When the workflow runs in a fork, `${{ secrets.OPENCLAW_DOCS_SYNC_TOKEN }}` expands to an empty string, so the clone/push URL becomes `https://x-access-token:@github.com/openclaw/docs.git/` and GitHub rejects it with `Invalid username or token. Password authentication is not supported for Git operations.` - **Missing detection / guardrail:** No `if: github.repository == 'openclaw/openclaw'` on the job, even though the same repository relies on that exact check in ~30 other places to keep fork runs safe. - **Contributing context:** The workflows were likely adapted from local-only automation; the push-to-external-repo step is what makes the fork-guard a requirement rather than a nicety. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [ ] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient - **Target test or file:** N/A — GitHub Actions `if:` expressions are evaluated by GitHub's runner and are not covered by the project's test suites. The guard syntax itself is validated by GitHub's workflow parser. - **Scenario the test should lock in:** On fork `main` push, the two docs workflows must evaluate to Skipped rather than running. - **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** A job-level `if:` is the idiomatic, zero-cost mechanism GitHub Actions provides for this; the repo already uses it extensively for the same reason. - **Existing test that already covers this (if any):** N/A. - **If no new test is added, why not:** No project-level test harness exists for workflow fork-safety; the guard is the test. Closes #38543 ## Test plan - [x] New unit tests in `src/shared/chat-content.test.ts` (11 tests passing) - [ ] Verify vLLM openai-completions responses render correctly without nested JSON 🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-08T07:16:31Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR fixes a double JSON-serialization bug in `extractTextFromChatContent` by detecting when a string input is a JSON-encoded array of content blocks (as emitted by vLLM's openai-completions path), parsing it, and recursively processing it as an array — preventing downstream `JSON.stringify` from re-serializing the already-stringified content. - The fix correctly handles the common case (all-text content block arrays from vLLM) and is well-tested for that scenario. - **Logic gap in mixed-array detection**: The `every()` guard requires `"text" in item`, which means any JSON-stringified array that contains non-text blocks (e.g. `{"type":"image",...}`) will fail detection and be returned as a raw JSON string. The existing array path already skips non-text blocks gracefully, but this shortcut in the string path is inconsistent with that behaviour — dropping the `"text" in item` requirement and relying only on `"type" in item` would bring them into alignment. - The test suite covers the intended cases well but is missing a scenario for a stringified mixed-content array (image + text blocks), which would expose the detection gap.

Confidence Score: 3/5

- Safe to merge for text-only vLLM responses, but the mixed-content edge case can still produce raw JSON strings where text extraction is expected. - The core fix is correct and well-tested for the primary use case. However, the `"text" in item` predicate in the detection guard is more restrictive than the existing array-path logic, creating a silent inconsistency for mixed content block arrays. This is a real but likely rare edge case for vLLM today, so the change is not dangerous — but the gap should be addressed before closing the issue entirely. - `src/shared/chat-content.ts` — the `every()` detection predicate on lines 21-23 needs the `"text" in item` condition removed. github_context:

- State: CLOSED

- Labels: size: S, proof: supplied, proof: sufficient, P2, rating: 🐚 platinum hermit, status: 👀 ready for maintainer look

Body:
```markdown
## Summary
- forward `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx` into browser screenshot tool results
- forward the same image sanitization limits into `snapshot --labels` image results
- add regression coverage for both browser image-returning paths

## Problem
`browser screenshot` normalized images to the browser layer's `<= 2000px` limit first, but then returned them through `imageResultFromFile()` without passing runtime image sanitization config.

That meant the final tool image still fell back to the default `1200px` sanitize limit, even when `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx` was configured higher. The same omission existed for `browser snapshot --labels`.

## Fix
Thread runtime image sanitization limits into both browser image-returning call sites:
- `browser:screenshot`
- `browser:snapshot` when `labels=true`

This keeps browser tool image behavior aligned with the documented global image sanitization setting and with other OpenClaw image paths that already forward this config.

## Verification
- focused browser extension test suite passed:
  - `extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts` (`51 passed`)
- direct runtime check with the same input image showed:
  - default config: final browser screenshot output still reduced to `1200x625`
  - `imageMaxDimensionPx = 1920`: final output became `1920x1000`
  - `imageMaxDimensionPx = 2000`: final output stayed at `2000x1042`
  - `imageMaxDimensionPx = 3000`: final output still stayed at `2000x1042`, as expected because browser pre-normalization already caps screenshots at `2000px`

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed**: browser screenshot and `browser snapshot --labels` image results ignored `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx` on the final tool-image sanitize step, so a browser screenshot could still come out at `1200px` even when the config was set higher.

**Real environment tested**: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS using the real browser screenshot normalization path plus the browser tool image-result path with a local PNG test image as the input.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: ran the browser screenshot runtime check against the patched branch, varying `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx` across default, `1920`, `2000`, and `3000`, then inspected the generated image metadata.

**Evidence after fix**:
```text
live output from runtime image metadata inspection:
normalized browser layer: 2000x1042, 246537 bytes, image/jpeg
default final output:    1200x625,  107491 bytes, image/jpeg
configured 1920:         1920x1000, 214162 bytes, image/jpeg
configured 2000:         2000x1042, 246537 bytes, image/jpeg
configured 3000:         2000x1042, 246537 bytes, image/jpeg
```

**Observed result after fix**: after the patch, browser-originated image results now honor the configured sanitize limit up to the browser layer's own `2000px` ceiling. `1920` produces `1920x1000`, `2000` produces `2000x1042`, and `3000` does not exceed `2000x1042` because browser pre-normalization already caps that path.

**What was not tested**: a full remote provider call was not run; this proof covers the local browser screenshot normalization path and the actual browser tool image packaging and sanitize path.

```

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

- add `node-gyp` as a root dev dependency
- update `pnpm-lock.yaml` with the resolved `node-gyp` dependency graph
- add a changelog entry for the local install/bootstrap fix

## Why this is needed

`sharp` can fall back to a source build during local install, especially when it detects a local/global `libvips` setup or a Node/runtime combination without a usable prebuilt binary. In that path, `sharp` invokes `node-gyp` from the workspace dependency graph.

Without a declared `node-gyp`, repo bootstrap commands can fail before OpenClaw code runs. I hit this while running `pnpm docs:list`; dependency hydration reached `sharp` postinstall and failed with:

```text
node_modules/sharp install: sharp: Attempting to build from source via node-gyp
node_modules/sharp install: sharp: Found node-addon-api 8.7.0
node_modules/sharp install: sharp: Please add node-gyp to your dependencies
node_modules/sharp install: Failed
[ELIFECYCLE] Command failed with exit code 1.
```

Declaring `node-gyp` in root `devDependencies` keeps this native-build fallback reproducible and avoids relying on a global `node-gyp` installation.

Fixes #84656.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm install`
- [x] `pnpm docs:list`
- [x] `pnpm exec node-gyp --version`
- [x] `git diff --check`
- [x] `node scripts/check-changelog-attributions.mjs`

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Local bootstrap no longer fails when native package postinstall scripts need `node-gyp`; `node-gyp` is provided by the workspace dependency graph.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS local checkout, branch `codex/add-node-gyp-build-helper`, Node `v25.1.0`, pnpm `11.1.0`, PR head `808978e1ef`.
- **Exact steps or command run after the patch**: `pnpm install`; `pnpm docs:list`; `pnpm exec node-gyp --version`; `git diff --check`; `node scripts/check-changelog-attributions.mjs`.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output from the branch:

```text
$ pnpm install
Scope: all 129 workspace projects
Lockfile is up to date, resolution step is skipped
...
Done in 7.1s using pnpm v11.1.0

$ pnpm docs:list
$ node scripts/docs-list.js
Listing all markdown files in docs folder:
AGENTS.md - [missing front matter]
...

$ pnpm exec node-gyp --version
v12.3.0

$ git diff --check
(no output)

$ node scripts/check-changelog-attributions.mjs
(no output)
```

- **Observed result after fix**: `pnpm install` completes, `pnpm docs:list` runs, and the workspace exposes `node-gyp` as `v12.3.0` through `pnpm exec`.
- **What was not tested**: Full `pnpm check`; this change is limited to dependency metadata and the install/docs-list path that exposed the missing native-build helper.

```

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #72021: Short-term promotion: signalCount mixes daily/session signals with real recalls
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 72021
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/72021
- Title: Short-term promotion: signalCount mixes daily/session signals with real recalls
- State: OPEN
- Author: CSZ-w
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:source-repro, clawsweeper:linked-pr-open, impact:session-state, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Bug description

Short-term memory promotion (`rankShortTermPromotionCandidates`) uses `totalSignalCountForEntry` to gate minRecallCount, but that function sums recallCount + dailyCount + groundedCount. Daily and session signals (from ingestDailyMemorySignals / ingestSessionTranscriptSignals) are counted equally with real user search recalls. This produces:

1. **False candidates promoted:** entries with recallCount=0 but dailyCount=3 pass the minRecallCount=3 filter
2. **Real candidates hidden:** entries with recallCount=3 score 0.64-0.68, below the minScore=0.80 threshold, because the scoring weights were tuned to work with the inflated signal counts

## Root cause chain

### 1. Signal type mixing

```ts
function totalSignalCountForEntry(entry) {
  return (entry.recallCount ?? 0) + (entry.dailyCount ?? 0) + (entry.groundedCount ?? 0);
}
```

The dailyCount comes from ingestDailyMemorySignals which scans past 30 days of memory files and stamps every matching entry as a daily signal. These are NOT user search recalls but are counted the same way.

### 2. Score inflation from combined signals

avgScore = totalScore / signalCount. When signalCount is inflated by daily signals, avgScore rises. An entry with recallCount=3 and dailyCount=3 has signalCount=6, giving avgScore higher than a real recall-only entry with signalCount=3.

### 3. Real entries fail minScore

Verified data from short-term-recall.json:

```json
{
  "key": "memory:memory/2026-04-16.md:407:425",
  "recallCount": 3,
  "dailyCount": 0,
  "queryHashes": 3,
  "recallDays": 4,
  "totalScore": 1.367,
  "computedPromotionScore": 0.645
}
```

With minScore=0.80, this entry is filtered out. The entry IS actively recalled by user search, but unreachable with current scoring.

## Environment

- OpenClaw: 2026.4.24
- short-term-recall.json: 10,429 entries, 8 with recallCount >= 3, 2 with recallCount >= 3 AND queries >= 3 AND not promoted
- minRecallCount=3, minScore=0.80, minUniqueQueries=3

## Existing workaround

Three patches applied to compiled JS:
1. Light dreaming sort: prioritize recallCount over lastRecalledAt
2. Rehydration span: widen from +3/+8 to +15/+20
3. Make totalSignalCountForEntry only count recallCount

After these, the system honestly reports applied=0 - no noise promoted, but legitimate entries still cannot pass minScore.

## Suggestion

recordShortTermRecalls should weight recall signals differently from daily/session signals when aggregating totalScore. Or alternatively: separate the minRecallCount gate to use only recallCount (not combined signals) while keeping the combined signalCount for totalScore calculation.

```

dependencies, not temporary cache. - **Reliability**: Ensures the install directory is always writable by the `node` user, even if the browser isn't pre-installed at build time. - **Simplicity**: Greptile Summary

This PR moves the Playwright browser installation path from `~/.cache/ms-playwright` to `/opt/playwright` in the Docker image, aligning with FHS conventions and simplifying volume-mount persistence. The docs changes are clean and accurate. However, there are two issues in the `Dockerfile` that should be addressed before merging: - **Build-cache regression**: `rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*` is called inside a `RUN` instruction that mounts `/var/lib/apt` as a BuildKit named cache. Writes (including deletions) to a cache mount persist across builds, so this line destroys the shared apt lists cache on every browser-enabled build, forcing `apt-get update` to re-run on the next build unnecessarily. The line provides no image-size benefit since cache-mount contents are never committed to the image layer. - **Missing post-install ownership fix**: The original code followed up `playwright-core cli.js install` with `chown -R node:node` on the browser directory. The new code only pre-creates the directory with `node` ownership, but the `RUN` instruction itself runs as `root`, meaning all installed browser files are root-owned. This can cause Playwright to fail when it attempts to write metadata or perform incremental installs at runtime as the `node` user.

Confidence Score: 2/5

- Not safe to merge as-is; two logic issues in the Dockerfile need to be fixed. - The overall direction of the PR is sound, but the `rm -rf` inside a BuildKit cache mount silently degrades build performance, and dropping the post-install `chown -R` is a behavioral regression that can surface as permission errors for users who rely on Playwright's runtime install path. - `Dockerfile` — specifically lines 164-177 covering the browser install block. Greptile Summary

This PR fixes a bug where expired `.deleted.*` and `.reset.*` session archive artifacts could accumulate indefinitely in a session store running in `enforce` maintenance mode. The root cause was that archive cleanup only ran when the same save operation had also archived at least one live session — if no live sessions were pruned, stale archives were never touched. Additionally, older filename variants using `+HH-MM` timezone offsets or `YYYYMMDD-HHmmss` compact format were not recognized by the timestamp parser, making them ineligible for retention-based cleanup even when they should have been removed. Key changes: - **`store.ts`**: The archive cleanup gate is changed from `archivedDirs.size > 0 || resetArchiveRetentionMs != null` to `pruneAfterMs >= 0 || resetArchiveRetentionMs != null`. Since `pruneAfterMs` is always positive in practice, cleanup now runs unconditionally during every enforced-maintenance save. The `targetDirs` list always includes the store directory alongside any additional archived directories. - **`artifacts.ts`**: `ISO_ARCHIVE_TIMESTAMP_RE` is expanded to accept `±HH-MM`-style timezone suffixes, and a new `COMPACT_ARCHIVE_TIMESTAMP_RE` (`/^\d{8}-\d{6}$/`) handles `YYYYMMDD-HHmmss` legacy filenames. `restoreSessionArchiveTimestamp` now normalizes both formats to valid ISO 8601 strings before `Date.parse`. Note: the original split-on-`T` fallback branch at the bottom of `restoreSessionArchiveTimestamp` is now unreachable by any timestamp accepted by `isArchiveTimestamp` and can be treated as dead code. - **Tests**: A new integration test covers the exact bug scenario (cleanup fires even when no live sessions were pruned), and unit tests verify round-trip parsing for both new legacy formats.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- Safe to merge; the fix correctly addresses the cleanup gap with no risk of over-deletion for recent archives. - The logic change is sound and well-tested. The new gate condition (`pruneAfterMs >= 0`) is effectively always true, which is intentional — the `cleanupArchivedSessionTranscripts` function's own guard (`olderThanMs < 0` early return) ensures no unsafe deletion occurs. The timestamp parsing additions are carefully scoped and validated by new unit tests. The main minor concern is that the always-true condition is slightly misleading, and the original fallback branch in `restoreSessionArchiveTimestamp` is now dead code — neither is a bug. - `src/config/sessions/store.ts` lines 482–483 (always-true gate condition worth clarifying) and `src/config/sessions/artifacts.ts` lines 63–67 (now-unreachable fallback branch).

Comments Outside Diff (1)

 1. `src/config/sessions/artifacts.ts`, line 63-67 ([link](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/9d14a02307762e4420cde3f99df7ac0c863c1f22/src/config/sessions/artifacts.ts#L63-L67)) **Fallback branch is now dead code** After adding the two new branches (`compact` and `iso`), the original fallback path (split on `"T"` and replace `-` with `:`) can no longer be reached by any timestamp that passes `isArchiveTimestamp()`. The `ISO_ARCHIVE_TIMESTAMP_RE` requires the zone suffix `(Z|[+-]\d{2}-\d{2})`, and both new branches exhaustively handle every format that regex accepts. A raw value like `2026-02-23T12-34-56` (no zone) wouldn't pass `isArchiveTimestamp`, so it would never arrive here. The code is harmless, but it gives a false impression that there's a third format to handle. If this is intentional as a final safety net, a brief comment would help; otherwise it's a candidate for removal to reduce surface area.

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

Addresses the ACP `final_only` delivery-mode portion of #84486 — text produced before tool calls was lost because `resetTurnState()` cleared `finalOnlyOutputText` between model invocations within the same ACP turn.

Changes in `final_only` mode (all scoped to `deliveryMode === "final_only"`):
- `finalOnlyOutputText` survives `resetTurnState()` across invocations — pre-tool text is preserved
- `emittedOutputChars` and `truncationNoticeEmitted` survive — `acp.stream.maxOutputChars` is enforced as a per-turn budget
- `lastVisibleOutputTail` and `pendingHiddenBoundary` survive — hidden tool-call boundary separators are correctly inserted across invocation boundaries
- Intermediate `done` events skip final text delivery — accumulated text is delivered once at turn completion via `flush(true)`

## Changes

- `src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.ts`: four `final_only` state fields no longer cleared in `resetTurnState()`; `flush()` accepts `skipFinalText` option; `onEvent` passes `skipFinalText: true` for `done`/`error` events in `final_only` mode
- `src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.test.ts`: updated existing buffering test; 3 new regression tests covering multi-invocation text accumulation, `maxOutputChars` truncation, and hidden-boundary separator preservation

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Pre-tool text in final_only delivery mode is discarded when resetTurnState() clears finalOnlyOutputText between model invocations. Output limit counters and hidden-boundary separator state were also incorrectly reset, allowing truncation bypass and separator loss.

Real environment tested: macOS Darwin 25.4.0 (arm64), Node.js v24.14.1, OpenClaw 2026.5.19 (359135f)

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```
pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.test.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.test.ts

pnpm format:check src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.ts

pnpm lint src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.ts

npx tsx scripts/proof-acp-runtime.ts
```

Evidence after fix (regression/unit tests):
```
 Test Files  3 passed (3)
      Tests  100 passed (100)
 Format: all matched files use the correct format
 Lint:   0 warnings and 0 errors
```

Evidence after fix (real ACP projector runtime — standalone `npx tsx` execution, not a test):
```
================================================================
ACP projector runtime proof — deliveryMode=final_only
================================================================
Projector created. Simulating 2-invocation ACP turn.

[Invocation 1] done(toolUse) — text deferred, skipFinalText=true
  deliveries so far: 0 final
[Invocation 2] done(stop) — text still deferred
  deliveries so far: 0 final
[Turn end] flush(true) — delivering all accumulated text

----------------------------------------------------------------
Result:
----------------------------------------------------------------
  Total deliveries:     2
  Final deliveries:     1
  Contains "Step 1":     true
  Contains "Step 2":     true
  Contains "Step 3":     true
  Contains "Step 4":     true
  Final text preview:   Step 1: Planning. Step 2: Executing query.
                         Step 3: Results: 42 rows. Step 4: Formatting output.
----------------------------------------------------------------
PASS — all 4 steps in one final message, pre-tool text preserved
```

The standalone runtime proof imports the real `createAcpReplyProjector` (not mocked), creates a projector with `deliveryMode=final_only`, feeds it real ACP events across two invocations separated by a tool-call boundary, and captures the deliveries. After `done(toolUse)`, zero final deliveries occurred (text deferred). After `done(stop)`, still zero final deliveries (text deferred). Only on the turn-level `flush(true)` was all accumulated text delivered in one final message containing all 4 steps.

Observed result after fix: 100 regression/unit tests pass across 3 test files. Standalone runtime proof confirms that in final_only mode, pre-tool text survives across the done(toolUse) boundary, intermediate done events skip final delivery, and turn-level flush delivers one complete message. Format and lint clean.

What was not tested: Live Feishu/Telegram channel delivery with real ACP session (requires live credentials and channel setup); the Feishu-specific streaming-card delivery path (separate from ACP dispatch)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
```

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #71976: Memory Dreaming: Light sleep sort prioritizes recency over recallCount, hiding real recall data
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 71976
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/71976
- Title: Memory Dreaming: Light sleep sort prioritizes recency over recallCount, hiding real recall data
- State: OPEN
- Author: CSZ-w
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:session-state, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Bug 1: Light dreaming sort order (dreaming-Dmn9KbNB.js)

The light dreaming phase sorts entries by lastRecalledAt DESC first, then ecallCount DESC. Because ingestDailyMemorySignals and ingestSessionTranscriptSignals refresh lastRecalledAt daily for all entries (with signalType: "daily"), the daily-injected entries always appear first. Real recall entries (recallCount=3) get buried at positions 300-5000+. With the default limit of 100, the light sleep report always shows ecalls: 0.

**Current:**
``n.toSorted((a, b) => {
    const byTime = Date.parse(b.lastRecalledAt) - Date.parse(a.lastRecalledAt);
    if (byTime !== 0) return byTime;
    return b.recallCount - a.recallCount;
})
``n
**Fix:**
``n.toSorted((a, b) => {
    const byRecall = b.recallCount - a.recallCount;
    if (byRecall !== 0) return byRecall;
    return Date.parse(b.lastRecalledAt) - Date.parse(a.lastRecalledAt);
})
``n
## Bug 2: Rehydration search span too narrow (short-term-promotion-Cd3cMDbx.js)

elocateCandidateRange uses Math.max(preferredSpan + 3, 8) which is too narrow for broad recall entries (20-30 lines). When daily memory files grow, stored line ranges shift and rehydration fails.

**Fix:** Math.max(preferredSpan + 15, 20)`n
## Impact

Both bugs cause ^@ promoted results even when valid candidates exist in short-term-recall.json with recallCount >= 3, uniqueQueries >= 3, and score >= 0.80.
```

Comments/context:
```markdown
- CSZ-w at 2026-04-26T05:19:36Z:
## Environment

- **OpenClaw version:** 2026.4.15 (041266a)
- **Node.js:** v24.14.0
- **OS:** Windows 11 24H2 (build 26200, x64)
- **Memory store:** short-term-recall.json (10,429 entries) + memory/main.sqlite (564 chunks)
- **Memory search provider:** OpenAI / Pro/BAAI/bge-m3 via SiliconFlow

## Reproduction

1. Run Memory Dreaming Promotion (every 6h via cron)
2. Check short-term-recall.json: 8 entries have recallCount >= 3, 10,387 have recallCount = 0
3. Light sleep report shows only recall=0 entries (top 100 all from daily ingestion)
4. Deep promotion ranks 10 candidates but promotes 0

The root cause is confirmed via database inspection: recall=3 entries are sorted to positions 300-5870 because their lastRecalledAt is older than daily-ingested entries.

- CSZ-w at 2026-04-26T07:07:49Z:
## Deeper findings (2026-04-26)

After deeper analysis, three more layers of this bug:

### Root cause chain

1. **ecordShortTermRecalls signal type confusion** - ingestDailyMemorySignals and ingestSessionTranscriptSignals run before every dreaming cycle, writing signals with signalType: "daily" / "session". These refresh lastRecalledAt for all entries daily but do NOT increment ecallCount.

2. **	otalSignalCountForEntry mixes signal types** - sums ecallCount + dailyCount + groundedCount into one number. Daily-only entries (recallCount=0, dailyCount=3) pass the minRecallCount=3 filter because signalCount=3, even though they were never actually recalled by user search queries.

3. **Score distribution mismatch** - Real recall entries (recallCount=3) from user memory_search have totalScore 1.37-1.41 but avgScore ? 0.456. Daily-only entries have inflated avgScore because signalCount includes non-recall signals. With minScore=0.80, real entries score 0.64-0.68 and get filtered out.

### Verified data

`json
{
  "key": "memory:memory/2026-04-16.md:407:425",
  "recallCount": 3,
  "dailyCount": 0,
  "queryHashes": 3,
  "recallDays": 4,
  "totalScore": 1.367,
  "computedScore": 0.645,  // below 0.80 threshold
  "promotedAt": null
}
``n
### What needs fixing in source (not patchable in compiled JS)

- ecordShortTermRecalls should correctly distinguish recall vs daily signals when computing scores
- Or: 	otalSignalCountForEntry should only count recall signals for the minRecallCount gate
- Or: weight defaults and totalScore aggregation need recalibration so real recall entries can reach the 0.80 threshold

### Current state

After patching sort order (by recallCount first) and signalCount (recall only), the system honestly reports "applied=0" instead of promoting noise. The promotion pool is empty because no entry meets ecallCount >= 3 AND score >= 0.80 AND uniqueQueries >= 3 simultaneously. This is correct behavior - it just reveals the upstream signal weighting issue.

Unclear as a simple patch. - **Light phase still refreshes daily/session signals before recency-first sorting:** runLightDreaming ingests daily and session transcript signals, then sorts recent entries by descending lastRecalledAt before recallCount, matching the reported Light selection problem. ([`extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-phases.ts:1612`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/ca0fe884ff73/extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-phases.ts#L1612), [ca0fe884ff73](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/ca0fe884ff73))
- **Daily/session signals update freshness without incrementing recallCount:** recordShortTermRecalls increments recallCount only for signalType="recall", increments dailyCount for signalType="daily", and always refreshes lastRecalledAt. ([`extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.ts:994`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/ca0fe884ff73/extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.ts#L994), [ca0fe884ff73](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/ca0fe884ff73))
- **Deep ranking/apply still gate on aggregate signal count:** totalSignalCountForEntry sums recallCount, dailyCount, and groundedCount; ranking compares that aggregate to minRecallCount, and apply repeats the aggregate gate. ([`extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.ts:415`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/ca0fe884ff73/extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.ts#L415), [ca0fe884ff73](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/ca0fe884ff73))
- **Rehydration still has the narrow/drop shape:** relocateCandidateRange still uses Math.max(preferredSpan + 3, 8), rehydratePromotionCandidate skips candidates when relocation returns null, and apply returns zero when no selected candidate survives rehydration. ([`extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.ts:1460`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/ca0fe884ff73/extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.ts#L1460), [ca0fe884ff73]

[truncated]
```
